legalWitnessPolicy.authority: Transcription line ranges are the legal deed. Digest anchors are navigation aids only.

surfaceAuthorityOptionPolicy.rule: A visible Classical Authority option must have one unique complete Canvas tag with a valid Transcription line range and exact witness. Untagged, duplicated, incomplete, or out-of-range options fail validation.

statusValues.logic-tested: Rule logic exists and focused tests prove it.
statusValues.logic-partial: Some rule logic exists, but the tag is not complete enough to treat as finished.
statusValues.inventory-only: The transcription item is indexed for display or lookup, but the deeper rule logic is not implemented.
statusValues.needs-rule-logic: The transcription item is tagged and ready for a future rule-logic slice.
statusValues.transcription-review: The item exists as a lesson gap but needs direct transcription review before rule logic.

queryHandles.taggedButNotLogiqued: Find items whose ruleLogicTag.status is not logic-tested.
queryHandles.logiquedAndTested: Find items whose ruleLogicTag.status is logic-tested.
queryHandles.lessonSlice: Filter by lesson and then choose the first non-tested item.

items.title: Classical lane profile separation

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: Pronunciation. Orthography

ruleLogicTag.notes: Classical output stays in the Classical profile and does not use Nawat/Pipil spelling inspection.

items.nextAction: Keep as foundation for later lesson proofs.

items.title: Classical letters, macron vowels, and digraphs

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: In this text the spelling conventions used to represent these Nahuatl sounds employ the following

ruleLogicTag.notes: The current Classical lane accepts the core Classical grapheme inventory and blocks non-Classical Nawat/Pipil letters such as k and w.

items.nextAction: Expand only if a later proof needs finer phoneme categories.

items.title: Formula-safe boundary and punctuation marks

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: Several of these are used in digraphs

ruleLogicTag.notes: The proof allows Classical formula and particle punctuation without treating it as Nawat/Pipil spelling.

items.nextAction: Keep punctuation tied to the Classical proof rather than the old spelling bridge.

items.title: Spelling changes

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: 2.4. Spelling Changes.

ruleLogicTag.notes: Lesson 2.4 spelling changes are implemented as two rule operations: /k/ and /s/ environment spelling without pronunciation change, plus [w]/[w̥] and [kʷ]/[kʷ̥] syllable-final spelling with pronunciation contrast.

subrules.exactWitness:
/k/: ca co que qui; ac oc ec ic
/s/: za zo ce ci; az oz ez iz

This depends on whether the sounds are syllable-final or not.
[w]: hua hue hui; [w̥]: auh euh iuh ouh
[kʷ]: cua cue cui; [kʷ̥]: auc euc iuc ouc

items.nextAction: 2.4 complete. Next Lesson 2 target: promote 2.5 internal open transition into a Classical proof frame.

items.title: Spelling at internal open transition

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: 2.5. Spelling at Points of Internal Open Transition.

ruleLogicTag.notes: Open transition is implemented as a Classical Lesson 2.5 proof frame from the transcription, separate from the broader Nawat/Pipil output-surface rule.

subrules.exactWitness:
When two stems are joined by compounding
(see primarily Lessons 30 and 31), their boundaries, as a rule, are preserved by open
transition
an initial supportive [i] (see § 2.6, note) is frequently kept
a stem-final consonant has the sound it would have in vocable-final position

Stem-final
/k/ before stem-initial /e/ or /i/ is spelled qu

Stem-
final /kʷ/ retains voice on its release feature before a vowel and is usually spelled cu
At times stem-final [w̥] before a vowel is spelled hu

items.nextAction: 2.5 complete. Next Lesson 2 target: make 2.6 syllable structure a Classical proof frame.

items.title: Syllable structure

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: 2.6. Syllable Structure. A vocable in Nahuatl has as many syllables as it has vowels

ruleLogicTag.notes: Syllable structure is implemented as a Classical Lesson 2.6 proof frame. It syllabifies transcription examples as phonology and explicitly keeps syllables distinct from morphemes/morphs.

subrules.exactWitness:
has as many syllables as it has vowels (there are
no diphthongs)
A syllable may have one of four shapes
Any consonant sound between two vowels forms a syllable with the second vowel
Any two vowels in sequence belong to separate syllables

in these lessons it is never a vowel letter
but only a part of a digraph

No more than two consonant sounds can be juxtaposed, and such a juxtaposition is never
permitted in initial or final positions
Care should be taken with digraphs, since they represent a single consonant sound

any type-level consonant sequence
that is illegal at the token level is lifted into a pronounceable sequence by the introduction of an [i]

One should never confuse syllable
division in a vocable with the morphological analysis of a particle or nuclear clause

items.nextAction: 2.6 complete. Next Lesson 2 target: make 2.8 consonantal length a Classical proof frame.

items.title: Vocable stress

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: Stress in Nahuatl vocables falls on the penultimate (next-to-the-last) syllable.

ruleLogicTag.notes: Vocable stress is implemented as a Classical Lesson 2.7 proof frame that consumes 2.6 syllable division, assigns ordinary penultimate stress, records the vocative #é exception, and supports stress-group syllable division.

subrules.exactWitness: Stress in Nahuatl vocables falls on the penultimate (next-to-the-last) syllable.
Syllable stress helps indicate the presence of a vocable-final (reduced) short vowel.
There is only one exception to the above rule for stress
syllable division operates across vocable boundaries within the group

items.nextAction: 2.7 complete. Next Lesson 2 target: make 2.8 consonantal length a Classical proof frame.

items.title: Consonantal length

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: 2.8. Consonantal Length. When two identical consonants come together as a result of grammatical

ruleLogicTag.notes: Classical 2.8 proof frame handles identical-consonant grammatical construction, single bridging pronunciation, affricate release loss, double spelling, and traditional spelling warning.

subrules.exactWitness:
When two identical consonants come together as a result of grammatical
construction, they create a long consonant.

there is not a separate
pronunciation for each of the juxtaposed consonants but rather a single pronunciation
the release feature of the first consonant is lost

When a
long consonant occurs within a vocable, it is usually spelled with a double letter or a double
digraph

one should, however, be alert to the possibility of a long consonant
spelled as a short one

items.nextAction: 2.8 complete. Next Lesson 2 target: make 2.9-2.11 assimilation a Classical proof frame.

items.title: Assimilation

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: 2.9. Assimilation. When, as a result of grammatical construction, certain unlike consonants

ruleLogicTag.notes: Classical 2.9-2.11 assimilation is implemented as a transcription-first proof frame covering the general gate, progressive rules, regressive rules, partial assimilation, low-frequency assimilation, and the dissimilation note without borrowing Nawat/Pipil allomorphy. Lesson 2.10 also has a general morph-boundary operation that carries authorized progressive assimilation into Lesson 7 class and selected-output generation. The typed Lesson 8 finalizer applies the same operation after directional placement, including huāl + tlāl- > huāl(lāl-); Canvas examples remain proof anchors rather than a verb whitelist.

subrules.exactWitness:
certain unlike consonants
are juxtaposed
The assimilation may be progressive
1. /l/ + /λ/ > [ll]: mīl- + -tlah > mīllah
2. /l/ + /y/ > [ll]: pil- + -yōtl > pillōtl
3. /s/ + /y/ > [ss]: chōquiz- + -yoh > chōquizzoh
4. /š/ + /y/ > [šš]: mix- + -yoh > mixxoh
5. /¢/ + /y/ > [¢¢]: huitz- + -yoh > huitztzoh
6. /č/ + /y/ > [čč]: oquich- + -yōtl > oquichchōtl

The following rules deal with the most frequent cases of progressive
assimilation (the first consonant dominates the second)
Rules 1 and 2 above are the only ways in which the [ll] sequence can occur
1. /nasal/+ /sibilant/> [long sibilant]
In a combination of any two unlike consonants of the group /s š ¢ č/
3. /w/ + /bilabial/ > [long bilabial]
When /m/ is followed by /t/, /λ/, /¢/, /č/, /k/, or /kʷ/, it undergoes partial assimilation
7. When /n/ is followed by /p/, it undergoes partial assimilation, resulting in [mp].
other, less frequently encountered assimilation is possible
another process called dissimilation

items.nextAction: 2.9-2.11 complete. Next Lesson 2 target: make 2.12 consonant loss a Classical proof frame.

items.title: Consonant loss

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: 2.12. Consonant Loss. In certain consonant sequences one of the consonants becomes imperceptible.

ruleLogicTag.notes: Classical 2.12 consonant loss is implemented as a proof frame for optional tz/ch/glottal+w/y cases, glottal+y reduplication blocking, y-loss notes, nasal traces, and w+w loss.

subrules.exactWitness: In certain consonant sequences one of the consonants becomes imperceptible.
1. /¢/ + /w/ > [¢]. This is an optional rule.
2. /č/ + /w/ > [č]. This is an optional rule.
3. /ʔ/ + /y/ > [h]. This is an optional rule.
There is another rule that produces the opposite result
This rule cannot operate when the /ʔ/ is due to reduplication.
A stem-initial /y/ is sometimes omitted for no apparent reason
/y/ between the two long vowels /a:/ and /o:/ (in either order) is lost
6. /w/ + /w/ > [w]. The first /w/, which is voiceless, is lost.

items.nextAction: 2.12 complete. Next Lesson 2 target: make 2.13 consonant-phone shift a Classical proof frame.

items.title: Consonant-phone shift other than assimilation

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: 2.13. Consonant-Phone Shift Other Than Assimilation.

ruleLogicTag.notes: Classical 2.13 consonant-phone shift is implemented as a proof frame for glottal-before-vowel shift, intervocalic y disappearance, exposed m/y/kw/t shifts, m stress-group reversion, and the rare non-final glottal-to-t shift.

subrules.exactWitness:
a shift may occur when, as a
result of grammatical construction
1. /ʔ/ + /vowel/ > [y] + [vowel]. This is an optional change.

When intervocalic, the resultant
[y] may disappear.
2. /m/ > [n̥] when left exposed at the end of a syllable or vocable.
3. /y/ > [š] when left exposed at the end of a syllable or vocable.
When the vocable contains a prior s-sound, /y/ > [s]
4. Occasionally, /kʷ/ > [k]
5. Occasionally, /t/ > [h] when the /t/ is forced into a vocable-final position

non-final /ʔ/ > non-
final [t]

items.nextAction: 2.13 complete. Next Lesson 2 target: make 2.14 vowel elision a Classical proof frame.

items.title: Vowel elision

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: 2.14. Vowel Elision. An initial or final short vowel of certain vocables can be elided

ruleLogicTag.notes: Classical 2.14 vowel elision is implemented as a proof frame that gates short-vowel elision to set stress-group combinations, blocks long-vowel elision, records spelling-change consequences, and distinguishes supportive-i omission from proper elision.

subrules.exactWitness: An initial or final short vowel of certain vocables can be elided

A long vowel tends not
to undergo elision.
zā oc > zāc
if elision is indicated in writing, a spelling change is often necessary
When the omitted vowel is a supportive [i], it is not, properly speaking, elision

items.nextAction: 2.14 complete. Next Lesson 2 target: make 2.15 long vowel plus glottal stop a Classical proof frame.

items.title: Long vowel to short vowel plus glottal stop

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: 2.15. Long Vowel to Short Vowel Plus Glottal Stop.

ruleLogicTag.notes: Classical 2.15 long-vowel to short-vowel plus glottal stop is implemented as a proof frame requiring a small permitted morpheme class, compound embed subposition, and matrix-determined choice.

subrules.exactWitness:
certain morphemes with a long
final vowel have in their morphic repertory an irregular morph that has a short vowel plus a glottal
stop
Only a small number of morphemes permit this.
the morph with the glottal stop must occupy the embed subposition of a compound stem
its choice being determined by the morpheme in the matrix subposition
huē- + -cāuh > huehcāuh

items.nextAction: 2.15 complete. Next Lesson 2 target: make 2.16 prosodic contours a Classical proof frame.

items.title: Prosodic contours

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: 2.16. Prosodic Contours. Classical Nahuatl sentences, of course, had prosodic contours

ruleLogicTag.notes: Classical 2.16 prosodic contours are implemented as a proof frame that carries known stress and long-final-vowel low-pitch facts while explicitly blocking sentential-prosody generation for lack of information.

subrules.exactWitness: Classical Nahuatl sentences, of course, had prosodic contours
rules for stress are known for nuclear clauses and stress groups
a long final vowel has low pitch
practically nothing is known of the language's sentential prosodic features

items.nextAction: Lesson 2 transcription-first rule logic complete. Continue to Lesson 3 only when explicitly summoned.

items.title: Lesson 3 particle inventory

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: A particle therefore has no internal morphological structure.

ruleLogicTag.notes: Particle lookup is Andrews transcription direct and exposes actual particle entries. Lesson 3 is particle authority, not nuclear-clause authority.

items.nextAction: Use this as inventory authority, not as nuclear-clause authority.

items.title: Particles are not nuclear clauses

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: A particle therefore has no internal morphological structure.

ruleLogicTag.notes: Lesson 4 blocks if Lesson 3 separation proof is missing. This proof separates particles from nuclear clauses; it does not make Lesson 3 a nuclear-clause authority.

items.nextAction: Keep this as the firewall between particle output and nuclear-clause formulas.

items.title: Functional classes of particles

transcriptionTag.exactWitness:
Particles never serve as
a matrix stem.

ruleLogicTag.notes: Functional classes are implemented as particle-boundary rule logic from Andrews 3.2. They classify particles but do not authorize nuclear-clause formulas.

items.nextAction: Use these classes as particle authority only. Sentence or clause composition must be a later explicit target.

items.title: Negativizing particles

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: 3.3. Negativizing Particles. Nahuatl has two negative particles: ah# and ca#

ruleLogicTag.notes: Negativizing particles are implemented as particle-prefix rule logic: ah# elsewhere, ca# only after ma/tla/mah. This is no sentence/clause negation generation.

items.nextAction: Keep this as particle-prefix authority until sentence/clause negation is the explicit rule-logic target.

items.title: Particle collocations

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: 3.4. Particle Collocations. Very frequently two or more particles are combined in sequence.

ruleLogicTag.notes: Collocations are implemented as fixed-order particle sequences written separately under Andrews 3.4, with no compound or nuclear-clause generation.

items.nextAction: Use these as particle-collocation authority only. Clause attachment or sentence composition must be a later explicit target.

items.title: Honorificized particles

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: 3.5. Honorificized Particles. Honorific expressions play an important role in Nahuatl

ruleLogicTag.notes: Honorificized particles are implemented as tzin attachment to a particle or final collocation member under Andrews 3.5, with no honorific nuclear-clause operation.

items.nextAction: Use these as particle-honorific authority only. Honorific nuclear-clause logic remains outside Lesson 3.

items.title: Nuclear clause equals subject plus predicate

transcriptionTag.exactWitness:
OBLIGATORILY contain a subject
and a predicate

ruleLogicTag.notes: Subject plus predicate is a proof premise before formula authorization.

items.nextAction: Use as the base proof for later VNC/NNC lessons.

items.title: VNC and NNC formula selection

transcriptionTag.exactWitness:
There are two kinds of nuclear clauses: (1) verbal nuclear
clauses and (2) nominal nuclear clauses.

ruleLogicTag.notes: The selected formula follows clause kind, transitivity, valence slot, and state slot.

items.nextAction: Extend with new formula families only by adding formula frames.

items.title: Predicate inside parentheses; connectors outside

transcriptionTag.exactWitness:
Subject +
Predicate

ruleLogicTag.notes: Predicate stem boundary and subject connector boundary are tested proof premises.

items.nextAction: Keep state and valence outside/inside rules explicit when later lessons add fillers.

items.title: Personal pronouns

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: The true personal pronouns in Nahuatl are ALWAYS affixal

ruleLogicTag.notes: Lesson 4 now proves personal pronouns as affixal formula-position logic: nominative in VNC/NNC subject positions, objective only in VNC predicate positions, possessive only in NNC predicate positions, with no gender feature. Lesson 5 still supplies later VNC subject fillers.

items.nextAction: Use this as the Lesson 4 pronoun-position gate; later lessons may fill the slots but cannot move pronouns outside the formula.

items.title: Lesson 4 consumes Lessons 2 and 3

transcriptionTag.exactWitness:
With the exception of particles, all the other vocables in Nahuatl represent
morphosyntactic constructs called "nuclear clauses."

ruleLogicTag.notes: Lesson 4 requires Lesson 2 orthography proof and Lesson 3 separation proof.

items.nextAction: Use this pattern for later lessons: each new proof consumes the earlier proof chain.

items.title: Intransitive VNC formula

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: Linear format: #pers¹-pers²(STEM)tns+num¹-num²#

ruleLogicTag.notes: Lesson 5 now has a legal-witnessed formula rule frame and still consumes an authorized Lesson 4 intransitive VNC formula before filling slots.

items.nextAction: Keep transitive VNCs for Lesson 6 rather than widening this frame.

items.title: Subject positions

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: 5.2. The Subject Positions.

ruleLogicTag.notes: Subject person, case, and number are legal-witnessed outputable slots around the stem and tense core.

items.nextAction: Use the same slot discipline when Lesson 6 adds objects.

items.title: pers1-pers2 subject-person fillers

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: The pers1 subposition is primarily the locus for information concerning person.

ruleLogicTag.notes: n/ni, t/ti, x/xi, am/an/az/ax, third-person zero, and pers2 nominative zero are selected by subject, mood, case, and following sound. The i in ni/ti/xi is now represented as supportive-i rule logic with base morph, following sound, and exact witness.

subrules.exactWitness:
The [i] in ti, xi, and ni is a supportive vowel, required when there is a following consonant
sound.

items.nextAction: Keep variants as slot fillers, not as user-entered stem text.

items.title: num1-num2 subject-number fillers

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: 3. Subposition num1 is the locus of a "number-connector morph."

ruleLogicTag.notes: Indicative, future, preterit, optative, and admonitive number dyads are legal-witnessed outputable subject-connector logic, including admonitive t-in/t-ih. The i in num1 qui is now represented as supportive-i rule logic, distinct from regular c/qu spelling and from square-zero replacement.

subrules.exactWitness: the irregular morph [ki], spelled qui, has a supportive [i].

items.nextAction: Keep number dyads outside the predicate stem.

items.title: Future singular square-zero subvariant

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: The irregular morph ⎕ is a subvariant of qui

ruleLogicTag.notes: Future singular indicative uses U+2395 in the selected num1 slot, with qui-0 retained as an alternate dyad; the square-zero ambiguity is tracked by number-rule logic. Square-zero now explicitly records that it replaces the supportive-i variant qui in the selected output.

subrules.exactWitness: The irregular morph ⎕ is a subvariant of qui

items.nextAction: Use U+2395 consistently for square-zero in Classical transcription and Lesson 5 output.

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: There are four possible paradigms

ruleLogicTag.notes: The subject summary is now represented as four legal-witnessed number-dyad paradigms; receipt inventory remains display-only authority.

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: The stem is the organizing center of the predicate, but until the

items.title: Receipt display is not rule authority

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: The simplest VNC formula is the intransitive

items.title: Transitive VNC formulas differ by Valence position

transcriptionTag.exactWitness:
The two transitive formulas given in § 4.5 differ from the
intransitive formula only in the Valence position

ruleLogicTag.notes: Lesson 6 now has legal-witnessed transitive VNC formula frames for monadic +va and dyadic +va1-va2; it consumes Lesson 4 transitive VNC authority and Lesson 5 subject/tense fillers.

items.nextAction: Keep this as object-pronoun authority; later double-object and derivational prominence changes belong to later lessons.

items.title: Objective object-pronoun categories

transcriptionTag.exactWitness:
The case category
here exploits the feature objective.

ruleLogicTag.notes: Lesson 6 proves objective case plus trajectory, specificity, and prominence before selecting an object valence filler.

items.nextAction: Do not treat object text as enough; it must carry objective category logic.

items.title: Monadic Valence position

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: Linear format: #pers¹-pers²+va(STEM)tns+num¹-num²#

ruleLogicTag.notes: Monadic +va is legal-witnessed for ne, te, and tla only, with te/tla treated as nonspecific indefinite-pronoun objects.

items.nextAction: Keep shuntline reflexive ne distinct from mainline reflexive va1-va2.

items.title: Dyadic Valence position

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: Linear format: #pers¹-pers²+va¹-va²(STEM)tns+num¹-num²#.

ruleLogicTag.notes: Dyadic +va1-va2 is legal-witnessed for specific mainline projective objects and mainline reflexive/reciprocative objects.

items.title: Projective object filler distribution

transcriptionTag.exactWitness:
Subposition va1 always manifests the category of person, but it is NEVER present as the
only information in the subposition.

ruleLogicTag.notes: Projective objects distribute person, number, and objective case across va1 and va2, including c/qu/qui, im/in, ech, and itz variants.

items.nextAction: Do not import Nawat/Pipil direct dyads into Classical selected output.

items.title: Mainline reflexive object fillers

transcriptionTag.exactWitness:
A mainline reflexive/reciprocative verb object reflects the person
and number information of the subject.

ruleLogicTag.notes: Mainline reflexive/reciprocative objects reflect subject person-number in va1 and carry objective case in va2 as o or U+2395 before vowel-initial stems.

items.nextAction: Keep mainline reflexive va1-va2 separate from monadic shuntline ne.

items.title: Mainline reflexive object paradigm

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: +n-o( ~ +n-⎕( = "+ lsg-reflexobj(" = myself

ruleLogicTag.notes: The selected-output frame exposes the mainline reflexive paradigm as n-o/n-U+2395, t-o/t-U+2395, and m-o/m-U+2395 according to subject and stem onset.

items.nextAction: Keep vowel-initial square-zero selection tied to Transcription, not Nawat/Pipil allomorphy.

items.title: Verbstem morphemic structure

transcriptionTag.exactWitness:
a verbstem is the locus for the
lexical meaning expressed by a VNC

ruleLogicTag.notes: Lesson 7 now proves a verbstem as the lexical-meaning unit; internal hyphens mark morph boundaries without turning internal morphs into separate glossed outputs.

items.nextAction: Keep stem-internal morphology as verbstem logic, not Nawat/Pipil spelling evidence.

items.title: Source boundary roles for hyphenated verbstems

transcriptionTag.exactWitness:
a verbstem is the locus for the
lexical meaning expressed by a VNC

ruleLogicTag.notes: Hyphenated Classical verbstems are one verbstem unless Canvas witnesses a specific embed/matrix relationship. The Fuente route cannot turn a hyphen-only source such as (tom-a) into a-embed/tom plus a-stem/a. The Fuente source-selection frame now lets Canvas select whole stem, internal morphemes, or embed/matrix, and accepts user-supplied parts only when they match a Canvas-witnessed source role. For open Lesson 7 source boundaries, the user defines embed and matrix in Fuente, then Canvas accepts or blocks those parts. Witnesses are proof anchors for variables, not a whitelist: solid spellings such as huelmati may resolve to witnessed huel-mati variables, nearby forms such as huelmatini do not inherit that boundary, and user-defined huel + matrix or body-part ix/ixi + matrix parts can be accepted only through their Canvas witness roles without rewriting the user spelling. Solid carried forms such as huelitta or ixchihua are accepted only when Fuente supplies the matching embed and matrix roles.

subrules.exactWitness:
a verbstem is the locus for the
lexical meaning expressed by a VNC
(tomi) = nonan, to become/be loose> tla-(tom-a) = to loosen/unties.th., to opens.th.
Nixōchitēmoa. = #ni-Ø(xō-chi-tēm-o-a)Ø+Ø-Ø# = I flower-seek; i.e., I seek flowers.

if an adverb precedes tla in the VNC, the tla is part of the verbstem.
Nitlahuelmati. = #ni-Ø+tla(huel-mati)Ø+Ø-Ø#
Nihuellamati. = #ni-Ø(huel-la-mati)Ø+Ø-Ø#

if an adverb precedes tla in the VNC, the tla is part of the verbstem.
Nitlahuelmati. = #ni-Ø+tla(huel-mati)Ø+Ø-Ø#
Nihuellamati. = #ni-Ø(huel-la-mati)Ø+Ø-Ø#
nichuelitta in notlahtōl ... For huel, which is an incorporated adverb here, see § 44.5.2.

+tē(tech) (chico-tla-mati) = to be suspicious of s.o., to suspect s.o. [As in: Motech
tichicotlamatiyah. "We were suspicious of you."]

tiquīximati = #ti-Ø+qu-Ø(ixi-mati)Ø+Ø-Ø# = you face-know him; you know him by his
face; i.e., you recognize him, you are acquainted with him [ < (īx)-tli-, "face" + tē-(mati),
"to know s.o." The incorporated nounstem is irregular in that it has an added [i], as if the
stem were *(ixi)-tl-.]

a stem signifying a body part can appear in the embed subposition ... tiquīximati = #ti-Ø+qu-Ø(ixi-mati)Ø+Ø-Ø# ... The incorporated nounstem is irregular in that it has an added [i], as if the stem were *(ixi)-tl-.
The causative stem can have an incorporated causative object.

items.nextAction: When adding new hyphenated stems, first ask whether Canvas witnesses the boundary role; do not infer embed/matrix from hyphens alone.

items.title: Verbcore citation form

transcriptionTag.exactWitness:
It is, therefore, important not to cite the verbstem
but, rather, the verbcore (i.e., the valence-plus-stem unit).

ruleLogicTag.notes: Citation logic now requires the verbcore when valence is present: te, tla, m-o/m-U+2395, and t-o/t-U+2395 stay Classical citation markers. Formula-slot placeholders such as va1-va2 are blocked as citation markers; specific projective citation uses a te/tla representative while selected formulas still use va1-va2 where Lesson 6 authorizes it.

items.nextAction: Use citation frames as valence metadata, not as direct surface generation.

items.title: A/B/C/D verbstem classes

transcriptionTag.exactWitness:
Verbstems belong to one of four classes, depending on the nature of
their perfective stems.

ruleLogicTag.notes: Class A/B/C/D logic is legal-witnessed as a relation between imperfective and perfective stem shape. Canvas example verbs are proof cases used against the rule logic; their identity is not itself runtime class authority and they are not a generation whitelist. Exact runtime authority is reserved for Canvas statements framed as exceptions or closed enumerations, such as the pāca exception and the Class D set. Unknown forms remain undetermined instead of acquiring Canvas authority from an example or the default operational class. The complete table, not an earlier lesson shortcut, finalizes vowel length: A-2 present and singular nonpast optative are short, plural nonpast optative is long, and all nonpast admonitives are short; Class C present is full short, future is truncated long, nonpast optative singular is truncated short and plural is truncated long; Class D present is short, imperfect and future are long, singular nonpast optative is short, and plural/past optative are long. Hostile predicate carriers that contradict those Canvas cells block instead of reaching selected output.

items.title: Tla fusion as derivation

transcriptionTag.exactWitness:

ruleLogicTag.notes: Tla fusion now controls the selected VNC path: fused tla builds a derived intransitive verbstem before class and tense selection, unfused tla stays in the Lesson 6 transitive object slot, and Canvas's adverb-before-tla boundary test blocks hostile opposite analyses. The rule frame now exposes the general rule, variables, inputs, outputs, and proof anchors; witnesses prove the variables but do not whitelist every similar-looking surface. When fusion is selected, the rule itself supplies the source +tla valence and the output remains a derived intransitive verbstem, so matrix-only stems such as temō can build tla-temō without borrowing the huel-mati boundary witness. For open compound cases, Fuente supplies user-defined embed and matrix parts; Canvas-permitted huel + matrix builds huel-la-MATRIX and Canvas-permitted body-part ix/ixi + matrix builds USER-SPELLED-EMBED-tla-MATRIX. This applies to solid carried forms only after Fuente defines the roles, so ixchihua plus Fuente ix/chihua can build ix-tla-chihua while ixchihua alone stays a whole/internal source.

items.title: Expanded VNC boundary: true directional expansion versus outside particles

It carries directional placement into selected output, keeps ō#/ah#/ca# outside the VNC formula, preserves object shape under outside prefixes, and blocks hostile attempts to use outside particles as formula slots.

subrules.exactWitness: The prefix functions as an adverbial modifier of the predicate and is therefore positioned inside it, forming part of the core.
If the transitive VNC has a dyadic valence position filled with a specific projective pronoun, the direction prefix is placed after the valence position

Since the particle lies outside the boundary of the VNC formula, it has no impact on the shape or spelling of the third-person objective-case morph in the va1 subposition.
The negative particle ah#, "not" ... stands outside the leftward boundary of a VNC or one of the VNC's fore-lying clausemates.

items.title: Sentence-surface use of the VNC result

ruleLogicTag.notes: Lesson 8.2-8.6 now uses the selected VNC result as the base for sentence surface: affirmative assertions require an indicative VNC, negative assertions add ah# outside the VNC, emphatic assertions add sentence-left ca, and yes/no questions use either question punctuation/intonation or sentence-left cuix. These particles are sentence-surface material only and cannot become VNC formula slots.

sentence.

items.title: Optative wish, command, future-command, and negative sentence layer

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: Mā nicochi.

ruleLogicTag.notes: Lesson 9 now consumes the lower selected VNC result as provisional material and finalizes optative wish/command/exhortation sentence surface at the highest active sentence layer. Introductory mā/tlā remain sentence-left particles outside the VNC formula. First/third-person optative wish or command surfaces require mā/tlā; second-person direct command may omit it only in nonpast optative commands because x/xi already marks optative force. Command/exhortation role is derived from subject person: second person direct command, third person indirect command, and first person exhortation. Negative wishes and commands use ca# when mā/tlā licenses it; a brusque second-person command without mā keeps ah#. Future and preterit optative uses remain optative by use while borrowing indicative VNC form; future optative command requires mā/tlā, and preterit optative obligatorily carries the antecessive-order prefix ō#.

subrules.exactWitness: By substituting an optative VNC for an indicative one and inserting the introductory particle mā
The particle mā or tlā is obligatory if the nonpast-optative VNC has a first- or third-person subject. When the subject is second person, however, the particle may be omitted
A wish sentence with a second-person subject may express a direct command, one with a third-person subject an indirect command ... and one with a first-person subject an exhortation.

inserting the introductory
particle mā or tlā, and changing the negative prefix from ah# to ca#

a future-optative VNC
and a future-indicative VNC are identical in every respect

since the mā that is the prerequisite for the negative prefix
ca# is absent, the negative prefix ah# must be used
one uses a wish sentence containing a nonpast-optative VNC

items.nextAction: Extend this same sentence-layer architecture to Lesson 10 admonition sentences without letting later sentence force become VNC formula-slot authority.

items.title: Admonitive warning/advice sentence layer

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: A VNC in the admonitive mood does NOT prohibit or forbid.

ruleLogicTag.notes: Lesson 10 now consumes the lower selected VNC result as provisional material and finalizes admonitive warning/advice sentence surface at the highest active sentence layer. Admonitive sentence force is not prohibition and is not negative meaning by itself. Nonpast admonitive sentences require the admonitive VNC and perfective stem environment. The introductory particle mā is obligatory outside the VNC formula, optional nēn may follow mā, and negative admonition requires mā nēn while keeping ah# instead of Lesson 9 ca#. Subject-person comparisons are exposed as roles only: second person resembles direct command, third person resembles indirect command, and first person resembles exhortation, but all remain admonitive warnings. Phase 2 has begun: 10.1 is now machine-readable as positive cautionary warning/advice, rejects the vetitive/prohibition reading, and marks English don't/may-not translations as not-authority. 10.2 now consumes the lower VNC/predicate proof to expose nonpast-only tense authority, perfective-stem basis, Class A h vs preterit 0 contrast, non-Class-A 0 contrast, admonitive number dyads, and no translation value outside sentence context. 10.3 now makes affirmative admonition conversion explicit: an admonitive VNC substitutes for a present indicative assertion, mā must begin the admonition sentence, nēn is an optional adverbialized-NNC strengthener, mā nēn is Canvas-separated but traditionally solid manen, subject-person comparisons remain roles, and hostile don't/may-not readings block at the Lesson 10 sentence layer. 10.4 now makes negative admonition its own transformation from a negative present-indicative assertion: ah# is affixed to the admonitive VNC, mā nēn obligatorily introduces the sentence, the force is cancellation of warning / recommendation to reject caution, the traditional positive-vetative label is not authority, and hostile Lesson 9 ca# logic blocks. 10.5 now records the admonitive/nonpast-optative/present-indicative/preterit-indicative contrast set, x/xi second-person optative distinction, plural-subject distinctiveness, mā as sentence-layer distinguisher, glottal-stop omission ambiguity, h tense-vs-num1 role contrast, and the nonpast block against antecessive-order prefix readings.

phase2SublessonStatus.hostileProof: positive admonitive cannot authorize prohibition, negative-command, don't, or may-not readings
forced future tense blocks at the Lesson 10 sentence layer while the lower VNC remains only provisional
positive admonition blocks requested don't and may-not translation readings while lower VNC proof remains provisional
negative admonition blocks requested ca# and blocks positive-warning translation reading while lower VNC proof remains provisional
contrast readings and antecessive-order readings cannot authorize an admonitive sentence while the lower VNC proof remains provisional

subrules.exactWitness: A VNC in the admonitive mood does NOT prohibit or forbid.
The nonpast-admonitive VNC is built on the perfective stem of the verb.

By substituting an admonitive VNC for a present indicative VNC and inserting the introductory particle mā, an affirmative assertion sentence is converted into a sentence expressing a warning or admonition.
The particle mā must occur at the beginning of the admonition sentence.
It may be followed by nēn.
It may optionally be strengthened by the adverbialized NNC nēn, "in vain, uselessly"
mā and nēn, which are here written separately, are traditionally written solid: manen.

The admonition sentence with a second-person subject is like a direct command ... with a third-person subject it is like an indirect command ... and with a first-person subject it is like an exhortation
the translations need not be limited to the ones in these examples; any rendering that conveys a sense of warning is valid
A sentence expressing a negative admonition ... results from a transformation of a negative assertion containing a present indicative VNC.
traditionally called by the unfortunate term "positive vetative"
The negative prefix ah# is affixed to the admonitive VNC
The particle sequence mā nēn is obligatory in negative admonition sentences.
The negative admonition sentence expresses a cancellation of a warning. It is a recommendation to reject caution.
the introductory particle mā, present in the third example, indicates that the VNC is admonitive rather than indicative.
The pers1 morphs x and xi always distinguish a second-person optative VNC from an admonitive VNC.
The VNCs with plural subjects are always distinctive.
distinguished from them by the presence of mā.
In texts that do not represent the glottal stop, however, care must be taken
the h suffix is a tense morph in the admonitive and a num1 filler in the present indicative.
that particle cannot occur with the admonitive (as it is a nonpast tense).

items.title: Productive type-one causative final-shape and typed internal-morphology rules

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: (tomi) = nonan, to become/be loose > tla-(tom-a)

ruleLogicTag.notes: The engine accepts any authorized typed intransitive source and applies the Andrews type-one lattice from class, final shape, and canonical internal morphology inferred without requiring user-authored hyphens. Final-i replacement/addition covers the licensed Class A and Class B categories; exact tomi and tēmi evidence keeps only the witnessed replacement. Class A non-ya final-a sources receive morphological replacement; boundary-free Class A/B root-plus-ya, ā/ē-hua, i/a-hui, o-hui, ni, and hui analyses feed their more specific routes. The §24.5.7 ni/hui preference is recorded separately from route availability, with exact preference overrides for the documented exceptions. The documented pil-i-hui gap blocks type-one fallback. Multiple valid routes or ambiguous internal analyses require a typed option selection. The source subject becomes the causative object, the matrix subject is imported, and Lesson 23 routes all resulting object levels. Caller boundaries, targets, formulas, surfaces, and alleged derivational records remain non-authoritative.

the source-stem-final /a/ is replaced by causative /a/
the majority of destockal VNCs formed with (-hui)
causative object in the causative transform). The subject pronoun of the source VNC is transformed

items.title: Productive type-two causatives from nonactive bases and typed liā/huiā exceptions

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: tla-(chīhua) = to make s.th. > (chīhua-ło) > tē+ tla-(chīhua-l-tiā)

ruleLogicTag.notes: After higher-specific exceptions are resolved, every canonical signed Lesson 20 candidate in the hua, ō, o-hua, or lō families can feed the Andrews tiā map. The engine embeds the exact Lesson 20 record, applies the two-consonant restriction to ō and o-hua routes, and assigns target Class C. Boundary-free intransitive denominal ti and root-plus-ya analyses instead receive §25.5 liā; Class A intransitive final-ō sources with a canonical hua nonactive receive §25.6 huiā rather than a false tiā route. Exact temō, tlehcō, and panō evidence selects the attested direct or replacive huiā route; generic final-ō sources expose the unresolved pair. Chihua-lō to chihua-l-tiā remains an exact overlay and browser witness, not a source whitelist. The §25.1 yauh and huāllauh causatives are exact typed suppletions; huāllauh preserves its huāl directional target environment instead of flattening it into the stem. Active, passive, and impersonal source VNCs are distinct from any later target voice. Zero-, one-, and two-object sources become one-, two-, and three-object causatives through the shared Lesson 23 router; a fourth object is impossible.

subrules.exactWitness: tla-(chihua) = to makes.th.> (chīhua-lō) > tē+tla-(chīhua-l-tiā)

the causative stem is formed by means of liā or huiā
Causative transform: tinēchchīhualtia

items.nextAction: Add any further §25.4.8 lexical exceptions or unexpected nonactive bases only through exact Andrews evidence; keep the completed yauh/huāllauh suppletion and source-voice transforms canonical, and continue to forbid caller-supplied nonactive or causative answers.

items.title: Productive type-two applicatives and imported mainline object transform

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: applicative VNC is the mainline object

ruleLogicTag.notes: The engine applies the predictable Lesson 26 type-two families to any canonical typed source: final i, Class C final iā, consonant plus final a with replacement inside the base, Class D, transitive Class B iya, intransitive Class B eya, intransitive oya, transitive oya with explicit root-plus-ya versus whole-stem history, final ō with a canonical hua nonactive relation, explicit root-final-l plus o-ā, and signed prior causative o-ā or root-plus-ya history. Xeloa to xel-huia stays as an exact lexical overlay across typed one-object valences, and a lexical-key-equivalent productive xel-o-a result collapses into that one exact choice even when the input contains extra internal boundaries. The occasional §26.7 tl-to-t forms pa-tla to pa-ti-lia and tla-zo-h-tla to tla-zo-h-ti-lia are exact documented overlays that suppress the usual tla-to-chi rule only for those sources. Explicit intransitive denominal ti is blocked from direct applicative derivation under §26.3. Exact final-ō evidence selects an attested route; generic sources expose direct versus replacive alternatives. Unpredictable first-type iā, rare tiā, and unlicensed optional consonant changes do not generalize. Lesson 23 assigns newest/mainline and earlier/shuntline levels; the renderer only displays validated options.

items.title: Absolutive-state nominal nuclear clauses

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: The Absolutive-State NNC Formula.

ruleLogicTag.notes: Phase 1 target: State-versus-Valence authority, no NNC tense, complete nominative subject system, class-conditioned absolutive number dyads, animacy, discourse-time, and State restrictions. Lesson 12 output remains provisional until Lesson 14 supplies nounstem class and use-shape authority. NNC Subject Authority exposes person, animacy, number, and number form as separate Canvas categories; metaphorical use is a conditioned reading of animate reference, not a third animacy value. Literal nonanimate reference normalizes to third-person common number, while animate reference admits singular and plural person forms. The old combined subject and referent values remain hidden URL-compatibility mirrors and cannot authorize generation. The Authority-owned full-paradigm projection uses the 12.4 subject summary as an exact witness, fixes Source analysis, noun class, and selected subject animacy, enumerates the remaining typed option contract, and omits candidates that fail selected-output authorization; rendered formula strings cannot add a row. Result presents the same typed inventory as a finite map. Each form retains all contributing source-row IDs, its typed diagrammatic projection, conditions, and exact Canvas witness; the exact table remains a secondary view.

items.title: Possessive-state nominal nuclear clauses

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: The Possessive-State NNC Formula.

ruleLogicTag.notes: Phase 2 target: possessive subject dyads, monadic nonspecific and reciprocal possessors, dyadic specific possessors, person/number/case distribution, and silent/supportive boundary variants. Lesson 13 remains provisional until Lesson 14 selects general-use shape. The full-paradigm projection consumes the exact 13.3 possessive subject and 13.6 possessor inventories through typed Authority options, not through a fixture table or formula parsing. The finite Result map keeps Possessive State as one categorical view and simultaneously projects every authorized typed possessor map in the Canvas categories Monadic possessors and Specific possessors. Person and predicate number remain the plotted coordinates; the Result does not ask the user to select a possessor that the full paradigm already enumerates. Rendered PDF pages 122-123 confirm tē with long ē, relational tla with short a, third-possessive ī, the 3pl st2 repertory m ~ n, and short o replaced by square-zero before a vowel. Canvas §13.5 points the 3pl repertory to the nasal rules: m occurs before a vowel, m, or p; n is selected outside that environment, with the suspension points reserving further assimilated realizations. The single-form Authority surface therefore has no m/n control or unresolved selection; the typed following stem determines one Result.

items.title: Nounstem use-shapes, classes, and derivational number relations

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: Nounstem Classes

ruleLogicTag.notes: Phase 3 complete: lexical class selection is typed authority while stem-final form supplies guidance and hard compatibility constraints only. State selects restricted versus general use; truncated stems require an explicitly tagged ephemeral vowel and Subclass 2-C supportive-i repair. Lesson 14.3 is surfaced as a separate Stem relation authority: Plain, Affinity, and Distributive / varietal. The machine derives the reduplicative prefix from an arbitrary eligible source stem, preserves the entire derived form inside one predicate-stem slot, and displays source to derived stem with the exact Canvas witness. Supplied finished spellings are comparisons only, never a whitelist or authority. Full paradigms enumerate the relation separately from subject and number form. Absolutive plural connectors are class-governed lexical selections: Class tl admits m-eh or 0-h, while Classes tli, in, and 0 admit t-in or m-eh; a tli stem such as cal therefore cannot produce 0-h. Possessive common connectors follow typed class/subclass conditions. Lesson 14.8 is now output-authoritative: the user declares the ambiguity kind, supplies an alternative nounstem with explicit vowel length, and must select one typed slot analysis before Result can authorize. Rendered PDF pages 134-135 confirm both n-o(mī) versus n-square-zero(omi) and ī-0(mich) versus i-m(ich); long ō remains stem material and spelling alone never selects an analysis. Every final frame proves that all active layers were actually applied.

items.title: Higher ordinary-NNC conditions and sentence participation

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: Further Remarks on NNCs

ruleLogicTag.notes: Lesson 15 is user-surfaced through one mutually exclusive typed stem-operation record plus a separate typed possessor-reduplication selection. The surface conditionally exposes regular formation, suppletion with exact lexical stem and connector, secondary general-use formation with the exact tē/ti/t carrier inventory, analogical tla formation, and tl 2-A to 1-A reclassification. Result receipts expose the selected operation, exact target stem, carrier or connector, basic possessor, and automatic hu-ān boundary action. Rendered PDF pages 135-138 fix the letter and vowel truth: hu-ān has long ā; tlācoh -> tlāca, pil -> pil-lō, and tēuc -> tēuc-yō preserve lexical macrons; the secondary carrier is tē with ti or t blur, never short te; tla-mā has long ā in mā; and māi -> mā drops ephemeral i. Loose operation booleans, finished strings, formula poison, mismatched target records, and short te cannot authorize generation.

the possessor pronoun tē fuses with a general-use stem
The possessive-state predicate is downgraded to the rank of a restricted-use stem
A Subclass 2-A stem of the tl class may be reclassified as a Subclass 1-A stem
the pronominal possessor in the NNC as the nuclear or basic possessor

items.nextAction: Lesson 16 must supply its special absolutive-only pronominal NNC family as typed stems and connectors, and must consume rather than imitate the shared NNC layer contract.

items.title: Entitive and quantitive pronominal nominal nuclear clauses

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: Pronominal NNCs

ruleLogicTag.notes: Phases 5-7 complete: pronominal NNCs are an absolutive-only specialized typed family. Entitive and quantitive subtypes reuse shared NNC subject/State slots; internal plural n remains inside the predicate while t-in or silent-silent remains subject number; personal simple/compound, interrogative context, demonstrative invariance, indefinite ah compounds, and compositional quantitive matrices are machine-readable. Source structure now owns pronominal family, matrix identity, Canvas noun class, and entitive/quantitive referent category; contradictory Authority requests fail closed. Person-conditioned eh/yeh and eh-huā/yeh-huā source variants now determine available subjects without rewriting the entered stem. Short c/ch quantitive variants expose only their Canvas-authorized sounded t-in plural; silent plural remains tied to a long matrix plus internal n. Lesson 16 discourse consumes the provisional NNC formula and finalizes positive initial inherent interrogatives with question force, while negative or noninitial environments remove that reading. Single-form Result reads noun class and referent category from the typed pronominal slot and sentence type from the final typed sentence frame, so stale disabled dropdown values and requested statement text cannot hide or contradict (tl-eh-huā)-tl- and its information-question surface. Personal frames emit the typed cooperation partner required by Lesson 11.4.7; English pronoun translations, formula strings, and boolean cooperation claims are not authority. The reusable Authority-to-engine audit verifies every selectable pronominal state and pairwise ordinary-NNC interactions against selected-output authorization. Full paradigm and single form use that same option contract and typed selected-output path; neither maintains a pronominal witness whitelist, and the single-form display cannot be authorized or changed by formula or surface text.
ruleLogicTag.surfaceNotes: Rendered PDF pages 143-150 fix the contextual and quantitive surface truth. Doubled first plural is a conditional typed selection only for personal-compound 1pl and exposes ti-t plus its special people-group meaning. A real dependent clause is a separate typed selection only for tleh or ac-long; Result writes the principal NNC and in separately and does not infer the selection from tlein, aquin, or a printed formula. Human itlah requires an explicit special-situation selection and no longer receives an unconditional hidden true. Quantitive Authority now exposes source-owned matrix family, exact form quī/quih/qui/c or chī/chih/chi/ch, and predicate pluralization separately from subject Number form. Combined qui-ch remains short and cannot take internal n; normal internal-n output realizes long quī/chī before n. Loose booleans, loose quantitive strings, contradictory source spellings, and formula/surface artifacts fail closed.

items.title: Typed nonactive verbstem derivation

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: the nonactive stem always belongs to Class A

ruleLogicTag.notes: The live Canvas requires one exact typed nonactive stem plus its Andrews suffix family. The family label alone cannot manufacture lexical allomorphy. Formula and surface artifacts are explicitly non-authoritative. The accepted stem remains predicate-internal and routes as Class A-2 before Lesson 21 or 22 applies.

subrules.exactWitness: the nonactive stem always belongs to Class A

items.nextAction: Expand automatic family-specific allomorphy only from typed source contracts; do not infer an irregular nonactive stem from the active spelling or a rendered formula.

items.title: Passive transformation over a typed active VNC

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: the reassignment of a specific object to the subject function

ruleLogicTag.notes: Passive consumes an authorized typed active VNC and an authorized typed Lesson 20 stem. It deletes the active subject, prevents agent expression, promotes one specific projective object to the discontinuous subject slots, and vacates that object position. A reflexive source preserves shuntline ne. Intransitive and nonspecific-only sources fail closed and are directed to Lesson 22.

subrules.exactWitness: the subject pronoun of the active VNC never participates in the passive transform; it is discarded

items.nextAction: Lesson 23 now extends this typed promotion contract to multiple objects without weakening the single-object passive gate; later lessons may add stem operations while preserving the same object-cluster authority.

items.title: Impersonal subject and complementary voice gate

transcriptionTag.exactWitness:
The subject pronoun (person dyad plus number dyad) of an impersonal transform is
always an indefinite pronoun and is always third-person singular.

ruleLogicTag.notes: Transformed impersonal voice consumes the typed active VNC and Lesson 20 stem, deletes the recoverability of the active subject, imports an external referentially empty third-singular subject, and preserves the active formula valence. Intransitive, nonspecific-human, nonspecific-nonhuman, and reflexive sources are supported; a specific projective source fails closed and is directed to passive. Inherently impersonal VNCs require a typed lexical classification and retain the lexical stem. The separate tla-impersonal route requires an exact typed derived stem beginning with tla- and an intransitive source; tla remains inside the predicate stem and never impersonates an object-prefix choice. Formula and surface artifacts authorize none of these routes.

subrules.exactWitness: by nature impersonal and there is no corresponding personal VNC
an intransitive active source has an intransitive impersonal counterpart; a transitive active source has a transitive impersonal counterpart
The tla-impersonal VNC is an intransitive construction

items.nextAction: A future lexical inventory may replace manual typed classification and exact-stem entry; it must preserve these source gates and must not infer tla-impersonal authority from an object prefix or rendered string.

items.title: Typed multiple-valence object clusters and ordered voice consumption

transcriptionTag.exactWitness: sequence of verb-object pronoun morphs does not reflect the history of the addition of derivational

ruleLogicTag.notes: Lesson 23 object requirements are typed by object kind, person, directive/causative/applicative governor, and derivational level. Mainline versus shuntline status follows derivational level; linear order follows the Andrews 23.5 priority specific before reflexive before nonspecific and human before nonhuman. Incompatible specific objects are silenced by typed position rules, while passive promotes the eligible specific object and impersonal preserves compatible nonspecific or reflexive objects. The Lesson 21-23 Canvas layer consumes this cluster before the Lesson 8 final boundary. Formula, surface, and requested carrier strings are non-authoritative, and forged clusters fail closed. The 39-formula audit executes all fourteen formerly missing co-occurrence routes while preserving every Lesson 20 engine-derived nonactive match.

subrules.exactWitness: there are three kinds of verbstems with transitive valence: (1) directive, (2) causative, and (3) applicative
No core, however, can have more than three.
only one can be represented by sounded morphs
Specific projective before reflexive.

items.nextAction: Later derivational lessons may supply additional typed stem governors and triple-object witnesses; they must preserve this ordered cluster contract and may not restore string or formula authority.
